By suspending judgment or presuming nonexistence, these studies tend to offer purely sociological or psychological explanations for why certain individuals or communities hold beliefs considered fringe. Although the assumption that one can analyze a phenomenon socially without considering its actual existence has been a powerful methodological tool, it also risks a form of social reductionism: treating UAP solely as social constructs without acknowledging any independent social consequences their existence might entail.
